title: Week 1 Challenge — The Share Market Simulator
objectives: Apply Week 1 knowledge in a simulated investment decision
Calculate gains, losses, dividends and total returns
Evaluate a portfolio decision considering market cycle phase

vocabulary.definition: The profit made from selling a share for more than you paid. If you buy at $10 and sell at $14, your capital gain is $4 per share.
The loss made from selling a share for less than you paid. If you buy at $10 and sell at $7, your capital loss is $3 per share.
The complete return from an investment, combining both capital gain (or loss) and any dividends received. Total return = (sale price − purchase price + dividends) ÷ purchase price.
The collection of all shares (and other investments) owned by a single investor.

questions.question: What is an Initial Public Offering (IPO)?
options: When a company pays its first dividend to shareholders
When a company's shares are offered to the public for the first time, allowing it to raise capital by selling ownership stakes
When a company repurchases its own shares from the market to reduce the number in circulation
When two companies merge and their shares are combined into a single new stock
questions.explanation: An IPO is when a private company sells shares to the public for the first time through a stock exchange. It allows the company to raise large amounts of capital from many investors at once. After the IPO, those shares can be bought and sold freely on the exchange. Famous IPOs include Facebook (2012, raised USD $16 billion) and Alibaba (2014, raised USD $25 billion — the largest IPO in history at the time).

questions.question: A share's 52-week range is $42.10 to $61.80. The current price is $44.20. What does this suggest about the share's recent performance?
options: The share is near its 52-week high — it has performed strongly over the past year
The share is near its 52-week low — it has fallen significantly from its high over the past year
The share is at exactly its average price for the year
The 52-week range shows the share is unusually stable with low volatility
questions.explanation: At $44.20, the share is close to its 52-week low of $42.10 and far below its 52-week high of $61.80 — a fall of over 28% from the high. This context matters enormously: a share at $44 sounds different depending on whether it was recently $40 (recovering) or recently $62 (still falling). The 52-week range is one of the first things investors check.

questions.question: Which of the following BEST describes investor behaviour at the trough of a market cycle?
options: Extreme optimism — investors believe prices will keep rising and are buying aggressively
Calm and measured — investors rationally assess long-term value and make disciplined decisions
Extreme pessimism and fear — most investors believe markets will keep falling, many are panic-selling, and the media predicts doom
Mild concern — investors are slightly cautious but still broadly confident in the market
questions.explanation: The trough of a bear market is characterised by extreme pessimism. Investors who held through the fall are experiencing significant paper losses. Media coverage is overwhelmingly negative. Many investors capitulate (sell at the bottom to stop losses). This emotional environment is exactly why troughs are the best buying opportunities — prices are at their lowest, but psychologically it feels most dangerous to buy.

questions.question: You own 500 shares in Spark NZ. Spark announces a dividend of $0.135 per share. How much dividend income do you receive?
questions.explanation: 500 shares × $0.135 dividend per share = $67.50 total dividend income. Dividends are a cash payment from company profits to shareholders, typically paid twice per year. Spark NZ is known as a reliable dividend-paying stock — its stable utility-like earnings allow consistent dividend payments.

questions.question: Mainfreight's share price falls from $70 to $49 as the economy enters recession. What percentage has it fallen?
questions.explanation: Percentage fall = ($70 − $49) ÷ $70 × 100 = $21 ÷ $70 × 100 = 30%. A 30% fall meets the definition of a bear market (20%+ fall). For context, Mainfreight fell similarly in 2022 as rising interest rates pressured growth company valuations globally. It subsequently recovered strongly, demonstrating why long-term holders who did not panic-sell were rewarded.

activities.title: Simulated Portfolio — The NZX Investment Challenge
activities.setup:
You have $10,000 of virtual money to invest in NZ shares. You bought the following shares six months ago.

Holding 1: 150 shares in Meridian Energy (MEL)
Purchase price: $5.20 per share
Current price: $5.68 per share
Dividend received during period: $0.12 per share

Holding 2: 200 shares in Fisher and Paykel Healthcare (FPH)
Purchase price: $21.40 per share
Current price: $19.80 per share
Dividend received during period: $0.19 per share

Holding 3: 80 shares in Mainfreight (MFT)
Purchase price: $64.50 per share
Current price: $71.20 per share
Dividend received during period: $0.75 per share

Remaining cash: $1,150 (invested $8,850 total in shares)
activities.challenge:
1. Calculate the current market value of each holding.
2. Calculate the capital gain or loss on each holding (current value minus purchase cost).
3. Calculate the dividend income from each holding.
4. Calculate the total return on the portfolio: total current value + dividends − original investment.
5. Calculate the percentage total return on the original $10,000.
6. Which holding performed best in percentage terms? Show your working.
7. The market has just entered a bull phase. You have $1,150 cash. Which one holding would you add to and why — considering what you learned about market cycles?
hints: Q1: Current value = current price × number of shares for each holding.
Q4: Total return = (sum of current values + all dividends + cash) − original $10,000.
Q6: Compare % return for each: (current price − purchase price + dividend) ÷ purchase price × 100.
activities.solution:
1. Current market values:
MEL: 150 × $5.68 = $852.00
FPH: 200 × $19.80 = $3,960.00
MFT: 80 × $71.20 = $5,696.00
Total shares value: $10,508.00

2. Capital gains/losses:
MEL: 150 × ($5.68 − $5.20) = 150 × $0.48 = +$72.00
FPH: 200 × ($19.80 − $21.40) = 200 × (−$1.60) = −$320.00
MFT: 80 × ($71.20 − $64.50) = 80 × $6.70 = +$536.00

3. Dividend income:
MEL: 150 × $0.12 = $18.00
FPH: 200 × $0.19 = $38.00
MFT: 80 × $0.75 = $60.00
Total dividends: $116.00

4. Total portfolio value:
Shares: $10,508.00 + dividends: $116.00 + cash: $1,150.00 = $11,774.00
Original investment: $10,000.00
Total dollar return: $11,774.00 − $10,000.00 = $1,774.00

5. Percentage total return: $1,774 ÷ $10,000 × 100 = 17.74% in 6 months

6. Best performing holding:
MEL: ($0.48 + $0.12) ÷ $5.20 × 100 = $0.60 ÷ $5.20 × 100 = 11.5%
FPH: (−$1.60 + $0.19) ÷ $21.40 × 100 = −$1.41 ÷ $21.40 × 100 = −6.6%
MFT: ($6.70 + $0.75) ÷ $64.50 × 100 = $7.45 ÷ $64.50 × 100 = 11.5%
MEL and MFT tied at 11.5%. FPH was the weakest at −6.6%.

7. Which to add to in a bull phase: Good argument for MFT. Mainfreight is a growth company whose earnings are closely tied to economic activity — it tends to perform well when the economy is expanding (bull phase). It also has the strongest momentum of the three holdings. Alternatively, buying more FPH at its lower price ($19.80 vs $21.40 purchase price) is a contrarian strategy — adding to a fallen stock in a bull phase if you believe in the company's long-term story.

activities.title: What Did You Learn From the Simulation?
activities.prompt: Looking at your portfolio results: FPH lost money despite being a world-class company. Mainfreight gained despite being a simple transport business. What does this tell you about the relationship between company quality and short-term share price performance? What would you do differently if you were managing this portfolio for the next 6 months?
activities.nceaLink: Extra Learning — simulated portfolios build the intuition that real money takes years to develop.
